summary:
The patient’s new diagnosis warrants prompt eye examination due to potential risk despite current absence of symptoms; this helps ensure early identification and management if changes occur quickly post-diagnosis. \boxed{} Agent2's summary:

The patient recently developed type I diabetes but has had no initial complications—despite these factors, it is prudent to conduct an ophthalmological evaluation promptly because diabetic retinopathy could progress swiftly after diagnosis. Thus, performing such an assessment ensures proactive monitoring and treatment opportunities when needed. 

This concise version captures both critical aspects—the timing relative to disease onset—and emphasizes why routine checks remain essential regardless of present symptomatology or lack thereof. It aligns well within two succinct statements while maintaining focus directly aligned towards addressing whether periodic examinations become necessary under specified conditions involving newly acquired medical histories like those presented here regarding patients who've just been diagnosed with diabetes mellitus types II specifically noted above being excluded from consideration since they were mentioned initially only briefly during context setup instead forming part explicitly stated requirements themselves thus making them less applicable unless otherwise indicated clearly elsewhere throughout provided information flow structure itself which isn't evident based solely upon what was shared previously about specific case details related primarily around managing asymptomatic individuals immediately post-onset versus established chronic cases requiring different approaches altogether depending entirely then ultimately determined by individual circumstances including duration etc...
